title: Three Men And A Comic Book

jokes.text: Good comics? You only buy Casper the Wimpy Ghost.
jokes.setup: Bart's urgency about getting good comics
jokes.punchline: Marge points out his consistently poor taste
jokes.explanation: Strong character moment - Marge's practical deflation of Bart's enthusiasm. Good economy and structure.

jokes.text: Equating friendliness with wimpiness... will keep you from achieving popularity.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's overly intellectual analysis of a cartoon ghost
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa - applying academic analysis to kid's entertainment. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I think Casper's the ghost of Richie Rich. -Hey, they do look alike. -How did Richie die?
jokes.punchline: Bart's morbid curiosity about a child's death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark turn - takes innocent cartoon connection to morbid logical conclusion. Excellent economy and structure.

jokes.text: Perhaps his materialism led him to take his life.
jokes.setup: Question about how Richie Rich died
jokes.punchline: Lisa's darkly sophisticated psychological analysis
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy - Lisa applying adult psychological insight to children's character. Perfect character voice with shocking content.

jokes.text: He knocks a guy into the sun and says, 'Hot enough for you?'
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun delivered after extreme violence
jokes.explanation: Good parody of superhero one-liners. The contrast between violence and banal pun works well.

jokes.text: I stand corrected.
jokes.setup: Bart's example of Radioactive Man's wit
jokes.punchline: Lisa's deadpan admission of defeat
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - Lisa's intellectual pride punctured by genuinely terrible pun. Good character beat.

jokes.text: -Who are you dressed as? -I'm Bartman. -Never heard of him, full price. -Rip-off.
jokes.setup: Bart trying to get costume discount
jokes.punchline: His made-up superhero isn't recognized
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Bart's schemes backfiring. Realistic interaction with bureaucratic indifference.

jokes.text: Your spirits have imparted a glow to this old warhorse. I feel like Radiation Man!
jokes.setup: Comic Book Guy's pompous welcome speech
jokes.punchline: Gets the superhero name wrong while trying to be cool
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy - pretentious but fundamentally clueless. Good character establishment.

jokes.text: That's Radioactive Man, jerk!
jokes.punchline: Bart's immediate correction with insult
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - no respect for authority, immediate correction of nerd error.

jokes.text: I stand corrected.
jokes.punchline: Same phrase Lisa used earlier, now from pompous adult
jokes.explanation: Callback to Lisa's line, but less impactful. Shows the phrase becoming a running gag.

jokes.text: Get that punk's name. No one makes a fool of me.
jokes.punchline: Overreaction to minor embarrassment
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment - Comic Book Guy's petty vindictiveness and wounded pride.

jokes.text: Has anyone turned in a left Vulcan ear? We've got a utility belt, tricorders, a light saber.... Sorry, kid.
jokes.setup: Lost and found at comic convention
jokes.punchline: Absurd collection of sci-fi props but not what's needed
jokes.explanation: Good world-building humor - specific to convention culture. Nice catalog of random geek items.

jokes.text: My own idea for a comic book. It's about a dude who drives a school bus by day... but by night, fights vampires in a postapocalyptic war zone!
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous high-concept mixing mundane job with extreme fantasy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Otto character moment - stoner logic creating absurd superhero concept. Great contrast between mundane and epic.

jokes.text: The guy who played Fallout Boy on TV? I guess he wasn't killed in Vietnam.
jokes.punchline: Casual assumption that child actors died in war
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - Bart's casual assumption about why actors disappear. Good economy.

jokes.text: Laramie cigarettes give me the steady nerves I need to combat evil.
jokes.punchline: Superhero endorsing cigarettes as crime-fighting tool
jokes.explanation: Good satire of vintage TV product placement. Absurd juxtaposition of health danger with heroism.

jokes.text: Wish I was old enough to smoke Laramies.
jokes.punchline: Child sidekick wanting to smoke
jokes.explanation: Extends the cigarette joke logically - child character influenced by hero's bad example.

jokes.text: Who knows? I did play Rum Tum Tugger in Cats. Anybody see it?
jokes.punchline: Desperate deflection to irrelevant theater credit
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy - washed-up actor desperately trying to mention other credits. Realistic pathetic behavior.

jokes.text: Does Dirk Richter's ghost haunt the bordello where his body was found?
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate question about dead actor's sordid end
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy - child asking adult question about death and prostitution at fan convention. Perfect uncomfortable territory.

jokes.text: Dirk Richter was a beautiful man. Can't you vultures leave him alone?
jokes.punchline: Actor's emotional breakdown at convention
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to Bart's question - shows the human cost of tabloid curiosity. Realistic emotional response.

jokes.text: It is, my lad. But I'll let you have it for 100.
jokes.punchline: Agrees it's worth a million but only charges $100
jokes.explanation: Good Comic Book Guy moment - pretentious agreement followed by practical pricing. Shows his character contradiction.

jokes.text: But I must. I never knew why God put me on this Earth. Now, I know. To buy that comic book.
jokes.punchline: Divine purpose reduced to consumer desire
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Bart elevating comic book desire to spiritual calling. Nice structure building to absurd conclusion.

jokes.text: Your emotion is out of place.
jokes.punchline: Cold, academic dismissal of genuine feeling
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy line - clinical dismissal of human emotion. Excellent economy and character voice.

jokes.text: I never knew what Superman saw in her. Give me Wonder Woman. And that golden lariat. She can tie me up any time.
jokes.punchline: Homer's sexual fantasy about Wonder Woman
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer - inappropriate sexual comment about cartoon character. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: -Homer! -Just kidding, Marge.
jokes.punchline: Marge's scandalized reaction and Homer's weak defense
jokes.explanation: Standard marital dynamic joke. Good character voices but predictable interaction.

jokes.text: You really are a sport, Dad, taking us to a fine restaurant like that.
jokes.punchline: Bart's sarcastic praise for fast food as 'fine dining'
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Bart's manipulation. Establishes the sarcastic tone before the real request.

jokes.text: For a comic? Who drew it, Micha-malangelo?
jokes.punchline: Homer's mispronunciation combining Michelangelo with 'Mahalo'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer malapropism - shows his cultural ignorance while attempting sophistication. Good character-specific wordplay.

jokes.text: Well, T.S.
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual dismissal with mild profanity substitute
jokes.explanation: Good Homer moment - casual cruelty disguised as folksy wisdom. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: No! Look, we all know that usually when you bug me I give in. I'm not mad at you. Shows you been paying attention.
jokes.punchline: Homer acknowledging his usual pattern while breaking it
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-humor - character acknowledging his own predictable behavior patterns. Smart writing that comments on itself.

jokes.text: -Okay! -I win! In your face! Yeah! How do you like them apples?
jokes.punchline: Immediate victory celebration with multiple taunts
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the negotiation sequence. Bart's immediate gloating is realistic child behavior.

jokes.text: Don't gloat, Homer.
jokes.punchline: Marge telling Homer not to gloat when Bart is the one gloating
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - audience expects criticism of Bart but Marge targets Homer instead.

jokes.text: When I was your age, I wanted a child-sized electric light bulb oven.
jokes.punchline: Absurdly specific vintage toy description
jokes.explanation: Great period-specific detail - sounds like real vintage toy but absurd by modern standards.

jokes.text: -We'll give you half our allowance. -If you'll be our slave.
jokes.setup: Young Marge needing money from sisters
jokes.punchline: Sisters demanding servitude for money
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for Patty and Selma - exploitative even as children. Dark but realistic sibling dynamic.

jokes.text: -This gives us more free time. -Let's take up smoking.
jokes.punchline: Using free time to start their signature vice
jokes.explanation: Excellent character continuity - shows how their smoking addiction began. Good callback to established character trait.

jokes.text: -She means you should get a job. -Me?
jokes.punchline: Bart's shocked realization he'd have to work
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's entitlement. Realistic child's shock at concept of working.

jokes.text: I didn't realize it, but a part of my childhood had slipped away, forever.
jokes.setup: Realizing he needs to get a job
jokes.punchline: Overly dramatic narration about losing innocence
jokes.explanation: Good parody of coming-of-age narration. The melodrama over getting a job works for Bart's character.

jokes.text: -Shall I apply this to the cost of a squishy? -No. I need the dime.
jokes.setup: Bart turning in bottles for money
jokes.punchline: Refusing to spend his tiny earnings
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bart's desperate frugality. The pathetic nature of refusing a squishy lands well.

jokes.text: All those coins were worth three cents? Let the good times roll.
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic celebration of tiny earnings
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the bottle collecting sequence. Bart's sarcasm about his pathetic earnings works well.

jokes.text: Now you look pathetic.
jokes.setup: Lisa helping Bart look more pitiful
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact assessment of his appearance
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa line - clinical assessment delivered deadpan. Shows her analytical nature applied to appearance.

jokes.text: Form a line. Cheap beer and a sympathetic ear.
jokes.punchline: Illegal alcohol sales with therapy services
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from lemonade to illegal bar. Great combination of criminal activity with emotional support services.

jokes.text: -You got a liquor license? -My dog ate it.
jokes.punchline: Classic homework excuse applied to legal documents
jokes.explanation: Excellent adaptation of childhood excuse to adult legal situation. Perfect economy and Bart-like thinking.

jokes.text: My beer, my beer, my beautiful beer!
jokes.setup: Police pouring out his illegal beer
jokes.punchline: Melodramatic lament like it's a loved one
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the illegal beer sequence. Bart's melodramatic reaction fits his character's tendency to overreact.

jokes.text: You must be Bert Simpson.
jokes.punchline: Getting his name wrong despite it being very simple
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for Mrs. Glick - elderly confusion about simple things. Sets up her difficult personality.

jokes.text: I've got dried apricots, almond paste, sauerkraut candy.
jokes.punchline: Progressively more unappetizing old-person snacks
jokes.explanation: Good catalog of elderly person food. The progression from odd to disgusting works well for character establishment.

jokes.text: He was killed in the Great War. Held a grenade too long.
jokes.setup: Asking about photo of her brother
jokes.punchline: Casual explanation of gruesome military death
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor - matter-of-fact delivery of tragic military death. Shows how elderly people discuss trauma casually.

jokes.text: This one's for you Kaiser Bill, from the boys in D Company: Johnny, Harrison, Brooklyn Bob, and yeah, even Reggie. He ain't so stuck-up once you get to--
jokes.punchline: Casual conversation interrupted by grenade explosion death
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy structure - building camaraderie before sudden death. The cutoff mid-sentence works well.

jokes.text: This one's for you Kaiser Bill, from the boys in D Company: Johnny, Harrison, Brooklyn Bob, and yeah, even Reggie. He ain't so stuck-up once you get to--
jokes.punchline: Cut off mid-sentence by explosion, with specific soldier names
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy of death interrupting bonding moment with fellow soldiers

jokes.text: -No, thanks. -Boys love candy!
jokes.punchline: Aggressive insistence based on gender stereotype
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Mrs. Glick's pushy nature and outdated assumptions about children.

jokes.text: Filthy, but genuinely arousing.
jokes.punchline: Elderly woman's honest assessment of trashy TV
jokes.explanation: Excellent uncomfortable humor - elderly woman being surprisingly candid about sexual content. Good economy.

jokes.text: They've never improved on iodine.
jokes.punchline: Praise for outdated painful antiseptic
jokes.explanation: Good character line showing Mrs. Glick's old-fashioned medical views and resistance to progress.

jokes.text: -She doesn't have anybody. -There's a reason.
jokes.setup: Marge explaining Mrs. Glick is lonely
jokes.punchline: Bart implying she's unbearable to be around
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and structure - perfectly deflates Marge's sympathy with brutal honesty about personality.

jokes.text: I'll sludge you, you old bat.
jokes.setup: Mrs. Glick talking about sludge around downspouts
jokes.punchline: Bart turning 'sludge' into a threat
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - taking mundane word and turning it into creative insult. Shows his building frustration.

jokes.text: Some old lady thing nobody's heard about for 50 years?
jokes.punchline: Dismissive generalization about outdated things
jokes.explanation: Good generation gap humor - Bart's assumption about anything old being forgotten. Realistic child attitude.

jokes.text: It was my wedding dress. But then, I dyed it black and it became my mourning dress.
jokes.punchline: Reusing wedding dress for funerals
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about frugality and death. Shows Depression-era mentality of reusing everything, even for morbid purposes.

jokes.text: No, not the iodine. Burn the germs off with a torch. Amputate my arm. But not the--
jokes.setup: Getting hurt again and facing iodine treatment
jokes.punchline: Preferring extreme medical procedures to iodine
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and callback - builds on earlier iodine joke with extreme alternatives. Good structure.

jokes.text: -Here we are, two quarters. -Two quarters?
jokes.setup: Payday after week of hard labor
jokes.punchline: Pathetically small payment for extensive work
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the exploitation theme. Bart's shock at the low pay is realistic and sympathetic.

jokes.text: All right, off you go to spend it on penny whistles and MoonPies.
jokes.punchline: Outdated assumptions about what kids buy
jokes.explanation: Good period humor - Mrs. Glick's assumptions about childhood treats from her era. Shows generation gap.

jokes.text: MoonPies, my butt.
jokes.punchline: Bart's mild profanity expressing frustration
jokes.explanation: Basic reaction showing Bart's frustration. Good economy but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: -Really? -No.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming 50 cents was a lot when he was young
jokes.punchline: Immediate admission he was lying
jokes.explanation: Exceptional economy and structure - perfect setup/payoff with maximum impact per word. Classic Homer honesty at wrong moment.

jokes.text: Working's for chumps. I'm proud of you. I was twice your age before I figured that out.
jokes.punchline: Homer praising laziness as wisdom he learned late
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer moment - terrible parenting disguised as wisdom. Perfect character voice and anti-work ethic philosophy.

jokes.text: No way. What do you want? I don't need this. I've got a master's degree in folklore.
jokes.punchline: Invoking irrelevant advanced degree in defense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Comic Book Guy line - pompous academic credentials used inappropriately. Great character establishment.

jokes.text: I'd have thought an atomic bomb would kill him. -Now you know better.
jokes.punchline: Bart's matter-of-fact correction of logical expectation
jokes.explanation: Simple comic book logic explanation

jokes.text: -It smells like my grandpa. -Turn the page, Bart.
jokes.punchline: Uncomfortable smell observation ignored for comic reading
jokes.explanation: Good sensory humor - old comic smelling like old person. Shows boys' dedication overcoming discomfort.

jokes.text: I'll take 1 to 3, Milhouse will have 4 to 6... and Bart will take 7 to 9. -Wait a minute, what about zero?
jokes.setup: Complex sharing arrangement with random numbers
jokes.punchline: Bart catching the mathematical loophole
jokes.explanation: Good nerd humor - overly complicated solution with obvious flaw. Shows Bart can be sharp when his interests are involved.

jokes.text: In the event of a zero... possession will be determined by Rock, Scissor, Paper.
jokes.setup: Need to solve zero problem in random number system
jokes.punchline: Adding another random system to solve random system problem
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of needless complexity. Martin's over-engineering of simple sharing problem is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Nice try. It almost worked, but tonight the comic book stays here.
jokes.setup: Martin trying to take comic on his fake day
jokes.punchline: Bart acknowledging the clever deception attempt
jokes.explanation: Acknowledging Martin's attempt at manipulation

jokes.text: We want it to last forever... so the last one alive can be buried with it.
jokes.punchline: Planning for death and burial with comic
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation - taking comic preservation to morbid extreme. Good blend of obsession with mortality.

jokes.text: -What do you mean, the last one alive? -I meant years from now!
jokes.punchline: Realizing how his comment sounded threatening
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Martin's awkward backpedaling. Shows how innocent comments can sound sinister.

jokes.text: Well, nobody makes a sap out of Bartholomew J. Simpson.
jokes.punchline: Using full formal name for dramatic effect
jokes.explanation: Good Bart moment - using full name for dramatic weight. Shows his tendency toward grandiose declarations.

jokes.text: -Tell him what we do with squealers. -It's worse than when you have to pee?
jokes.punchline: Martin's innocent question about bathroom needs
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Bart's tough-guy act and Martin's practical concerns. Shows the absurdity of the situation.

jokes.text: It's not that far to the ground. The rains probably softened it up.
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual dismissal of friend's danger
jokes.explanation: Bart's cold calculation about friend's potential fall

jokes.text: If you hadn't tied me up, I could be saving the comic.
jokes.punchline: Blaming restraints for inability to help
jokes.explanation: Good irony - victim pointing out logical flaw in captors' plan during crisis. Realistic desperate logic.

jokes.text: I wanted Carl Yastrzemski with the big sideburns!
jokes.punchline: Revealing he wanted completely different collectible
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation - Martin's true collecting interest was different all along. Shows the absurdity of their conflict.

jokes.text: -What's your point? -Nothing. It just ticks me off.
jokes.punchline: Deflating the lesson by admitting it's just frustrating
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of the 'lesson learned' moment. Perfect Bart response - no growth, just irritation. Good structure.

tmdb_overview: Bart, Milhouse, and Martin pool their money to buy the new first issue of Radioactive Man, but their investment and their friendship are threatened when they can't agree on who will keep it.
insight: Three-way feud over rare comic escalates from bickering to genuinely dark places.
